title: Watch This for 13 Minutes and You'll Outperform 99% of People
description: In this video, a successful CEO shares his transformative journey from homelessness to becoming a monk and eventually a CEO. He reveals five powerful methods that helped him achieve incredible success.
transcript:
I went from being homeless to a monk to
a CEO. And these five methods help me
progress faster, be insanely successful,
and outperform everyone around me. And
these will drastically change your life.
Number one, the most important skill
you'll ever learn is knowing when to
quit. You know, we've all had that job
that drags us down or that relationship
that feels like a burden. For me, it was
when I was running the marketing team at
a company years ago and I reported
directly to the CEO, an amazing leader,
a coach. We would roll up our sleeves,
spend hours and hours trying to work
through problems together, thinking
together, he would challenge me, he
would make me better. And then, of
course, the company grew to thousands of
employees and the CEO had to hire a
chief operating officer. And from day
one, it was clear that the chief
operating officer was not interested in
me or in my input or in my growth. He
brought his right-hand person slowly
started stripping away more and more
from my plate and transferred it to the
guy that he had brought in. He would
constantly question my decisions and
undermine me in public. You know, I
started secondguessing myself, second
guessing everything. Hated coming to
work, constantly watching my back. The
culture was slowly turning toxic and the
worst part I started losing interest in
what I was doing. There was no passion
left. That's when I made the best
decision. In order to accelerate my
growth, I quit. I found another company
that was a better fit in terms of
culture and career. You know, my
personal growth just skyrocketed after
that. And here's the kicker. You know,
that favorite CEO of mine, he eventually
fired that COO. But if I had stayed, I
would have wasted years in a place that
was crushing my energy, my creativity,
my self-worth. We all have to learn to
quit faster. This takes a lot of
courage. I understand that. But the
faster you quit what's not working, the
faster you double down on what is
working. Here's a good framework to try.
I call it the quit quotient. Analyze
your current situation, not just for
work, uh, for relationships, for
projects, even habits, and give yourself
a score on three dimensions, each on a
scale of 1 to 10 maybe. First dimension,
drive. Does this work inspire me or
drain me? This is the personal
dimension. Second, fit. Is this the
environment where I can actually
compound? Am I with people who push me
to learn and grow? Are they investing in
me? This is the cultural fit dimension.
And the third one, growth. What new
doors will open for me here or later if
I stay? This is that professional
dimension. Now add all three numbers. If
your total score is greater than 24,
you're in the green zone. This is your
10x fit. Double down. 16 to 23 yellow
zone. The fit is unclear. Give it maybe
3 to six more months. Test again. Adapt.
Rescore. See where you land. If your
score is 15 or less, red zone. Wrong
fit. Quit fast. As fast as you can.
Because this is how compounding works.
How and where we invest our time now
will have dramatic effects years later.
The idea behind your quit quotient is
simple. If it doesn't fit, just quit.
Number two, the most important thing to
progress faster than anyone isn't talent
or strategy. It's your intrinsic
motivation. your inner why. Great
athletes know this. The secret of fast
progress, purpose and motivation. And
research backs it up and they show the
same results that intrinsic motivation
being pulled by meaning, growth,
purpose, contribution leads to a much
stronger performance and faster progress
than rewards like money or fame or
status. Howard Scholes knew this
firsthand. He grew up in the Brooklyn
projects. His family had no health care,
no financial security. That pain became
his why. When he was building Starbucks,
people made fun of him. They mocked the
idea of selling fancy coffee in America.
This is not Europe. That's what they
said. They thought it would never work.
But Schulz, as you know, wasn't selling
coffee. He was selling an experience. He
was building a place of belonging, a
place where someone from a project could
walk in and feel seen. That was his why.
It gave him the clarity and the stamina
to push through a mountain of rejections
for years and years. And most of us
missed this part. We chase promotions
and raises and equity and fame and
subscribers. That's why it can feel
empty when we don't get what we want and
feel empty when we do get what we want.
So now, how do you switch to becoming
intrinsically motivated? That's a tough
question. It's not an easy question to
answer, but I think it's all about the
internal story we tell ourselves. We can
try some small daily habits and rituals
that can take us back to our original
why insight. First, you can do slow
breathing every day to reset your
biochemistry. Some people meditate.
Second, you can exercise gratitude for
all things big and small in your life.
Third, you can visualize the future you
that's full of 10x potential very
vividly in your mind that works. I even
add a fourth step. I think about people
and send love and positive energy to
them in my mind. Do they know? Of
course, they don't. Does it change
anything in their lives? I don't think
so. But it changes me. It changes my
inner monologue. It really switches you
on and moves you to that positive state.
Top 1enters know this very well. Your
inner why keeps you in the game when
nothing else makes sense. Next is number
three, and this is controversial. You
should be seeking discomfort. Everyone
talks about the superpower of cold
plunges, and I agree. But the most
interesting part about the experience is
this. A systematic review of 11 studies
with over 3,000 participants found that
cold immersion does not reduce stress
immediately. The stress reduction
happens about 12 hours after the
exposure. I find that fascinating
because sometimes the real benefit shows
up way later. And that's true for life
as well. Seeking out uncomfortable
things today will train your nervous
system to be more resilient years later.
You know, in my final year of college, I
failed every single course. Like, I got
all Fs across the board. And what made
it worse was my early years in college
weren't great either. You know, I had
not developed a strong academic
foundation. So, I was lost at that
point. So, I would have to relearn 3
years of mathematics in one year to do
well in my final year or else I wasn't
going to graduate. Here's what I did. My
setup, a mat, a desk lamp, and a water
bottle. I sat on the floor cross-legg
for 18 hours a day, 90 minutes of work,
30 minutes of break, and a lot of
hydration. And that went on for weeks,
for months, for an entire year. That
extremely uncomfortable routine ended up
building my future for years. And more
importantly, it gave me this confidence
and clarity that no matter how bad
things get, there is always a way to
turn things around. That was a gift. So,
here's how you can use this method. Add
deliberate discomfort weekly if you can.
3 mile runs, cold showers, heavy lifts,
any task or project that you've been
avoiding. It's not whether you do well
or not in that task. It's about taking
that plunge. sometimes literally. Now
we're on to number four. The power of
networks. If you're not tapping into
other people's systems, you will always
fall behind. Madcaf's law says that the
value of a network grows exponentially.
So if you add 10 new nodes to your
network, then you add 100 more possible
connections. That's why the right
network can propel you forward in months
compared to years when you go it all
alone. Here's a true story. A friend of
mine introduced me to a design agency.
We hired them for a project and they
crushed it. They were awesome. The CEO
of that agency became a good friend. He
introduced me to one of his board
members. That board member later became
a CEO of a $600 million company, had a
great exit, became an investor, and just
last week he introduced me to the CEO of
one of his portfolio companies where he
has invested. And the company they
acquired has an investor who I just met.
See how the chain reaction just keeps
going? That's the exponential math of
networks in real life. It compounds
faster than your skills. It's not just
about contacts. The top 1% borrow other
people's nervous systems as well. Spend
time with someone who is calm under
chaos or sharp under pressure and your
nervous system will rewire to match
their behavior. Work with someone who is
extremely crisp and articulate and
you'll see that you start writing and
communicating better. That's the kind of
acceleration that you won't get from a
university or a book. Here's how you can
use this idea. One, make building your
network your top priority. Reach out
every week. Number two, ask for help.
Ask for connections. Ask how you can
contribute. And number three, ask
yourself, who makes me sharper just by
being around them? Double down. Spend as
much time as you can with them. Start
showing up with contributions instead of
requests. Your network isn't just who
you know, it's who you become. And now
number five is our last one. The final
unlock you've been missing is identity
shedding. You become exactly what you
see yourself as. Most people cling to
many self-limiting labels. I'm sure you
hear yourself saying, "I'm not a coder.
I'm not a leader. I'm not good on stage.
I won't be a good marketer. Excuses,
excuses, excuses. Your identity is like
a snake skin. You can shed it. You can
create a new one. The top performers
have always known this. They jump in
before they feel ready and that's what
keeps them compounding at 10x while
others are staring themselves in the
mirror. They don't like what they're
seeing and they're getting destroyed by
their own doubts. I have lived this
myself. At different points of my life,
I've worn different skins. an investment
banker, a musician, a marketer, a monk,
a mathematician, a CEO, a board member,
an investor. These are all various
manifested identities of the same
person. Was I really good at all of
those roles? No. Some identities fit
better than others, sure. But each time
I shared an identity, I gained access to
new skills and new rooms. Every 10x leap
was preceded by a 10x death of who I
used to be. You, yourselves, your
identities are all constantly dying and
are all constantly being reborn. Here's
how to use this. Ask yourself, what
identity am I holding on to today that
no longer serves me? Write that one down
and then let it die. Mourn for it. Let
the grief be with you. But after a
while, you'll see new skin coming on
like the spring season after a very
long, cold, harsh winter. And remember,
you will never feel ready to bury the
old you. You will never feel worthy of
the new you. But please don't fear
endings. They're inevitable. Fear the
beginnings that you never allow. Spring
doesn't ask for winter's permission. It
just shows up.
So should you. Those were my tips on how
to progress 10x faster than anyone. If
you like this video, please subscribe to
my channel for more videos like this and
check out my next video here. Please
keep showing up, keep growing up. Thank
you and I love you.
